IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act

sections.title: Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit

paragraphs.text: October 24, 2025

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The IKEA business is founded on a shared vision to create a better everyday life for the many people. As innovation evolves, we take responsibility for ensuring the ethical use of data and digital technologies in ways that serve people, society, and the planet. We support a European Digital Fairness Act that sets clear and consistent rules to protect consumers and children, prohibiting dark patterns and exploitative personalisation, guaranteeing price and contract transparency, and strengthening enforcement to create a level playing field for responsible businesses.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: IKEA is a brand that respects customers, co-workers and partners, while actively aiming to earn their trust. We are committed to include diverse needs and perspectives in the design of digital products, services, and experiences, and ensuring a human-centric approach to our digital touchpoints and data-driven processes. For Inter IKEA Group and Ingka Group, digital fairness is about how we design, develop and implement digital solutions that support people in making informed, transparent, and inclusive choices. We believe digital interactions should be simple and respectful, reflecting the IKEA values we have built our business on for over 80 years.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: People's data are not our product, and we do not sell information about their engagement in our omnichannel experience. We use data responsibly to improve and personalise interactions with our customers. When working with partners to provide analytics or marketing services we require them to meet high ethical standards aligned with IKEA's values. Where full alignment is not yet possible, we actively assess and mitigate remaining risks to safeguard people's rights and uphold our principles.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Many of our customers start their shopping journey online. While the majority still prefer buying in-store, we also want our customers to feel that their well-being is looked after in their digital interactions. Too often, digital experiences are shaped by manipulative design techniques that confuse or pressure people $^{1}$ . Hidden fees also make choices harder and erode confidence. A Digital Fairness Act would send a clear signal that hidden fees, manipulative design, and unclear contracts are not acceptable.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The EU has already taken important steps through the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), and other laws protecting consumers and regulating digital markets. Yet gaps remain, and enforcement is uneven. The Digital Fairness Act can improve efficiency by replacing fragmented national rules, provide clarity and consistency across Member States, protect vulnerable consumers, and create a level playing field for companies that choose long-term trust over short-term gains. Clear legislation, in the form of a regulation, supported by practical industry guidance and codes of conduct, can ensure consistent application across sectors without overburdening responsible businesses.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We design for transparency in how we show prices, contract terms, delivery options, and availability so customers can make informed decisions without surprises. Our global digital design system is built on the idea that all choices should be equally visible and easy to make. IKEA works to advance respect for children's rights in business guided by UNICEF's research, which has found that the commercialisation of children's online environments exposes them to manipulative design and marketing practices that can undermine their well-being and autonomy. Through the World Federation of Advertisers we support responsible standards for digital marketing and influencers.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: IKEA is committed to respect human rights wherever we operate, as set out in the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights. As called for in the UN Global Digital Compact and the European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles, technology should serve and empower people in full respect of their fundamental rights. We believe the Digital Fairness Act should be based on these principles.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Define and prohibit dark patterns that mislead, pressure, or manipulate consumers, including in consent, pricing, and contract flows.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Require transparency of all price components and calculation methods before purchase, while recognising that some variable costs (e.g., delivery or installation) can only be determined once the total basket is known. This should also consider financial services such as monthly payment options (e.g. this is offered through IKEA Financial Services to IKEA customers).

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Guarantee simple cancellation and clear consent so signing up and opting out are equally easy and transparent.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Protect children and vulnerable consumers by restricting addictive design practices and manipulative personalisation, and by setting clear accessibility standards.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Ensure coherence and effective enforcement with existing rules such as the GDPR, Digital Services Act, Digital Markets Act, Audiovisual Media Services Directive, and Consumer Rights Directive to avoid overlap and legal uncertainty.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
IKEA's priorities for a Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Focus on enforcement on persistent non-compliance while supporting companies that demonstrate proactive and responsible practices.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Pre-ticked boxes or misleading consent buttons that steer users into agreeing unintentionally.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Obstructed or multi-step cancellation flows that make it harder to leave a service ("roach motels").

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Fake or misleading countdown clocks and/or scarcity claims that reset automatically or create artificial urgency. Genuine time-limited or stock-based offers (e.g., clearance or "last chance" items) remain acceptable when clearly disclosed and verifiable.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Auto-adding items to a basket or initiating purchases through AI agents without explicit user initiation and confirmation.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Misleading or incomplete price presentation, including hidden fees revealed only at checkout, inflated reference discounts, or "starting from" prices that obscure real costs. Variable costs (such as delivery or installation) are acceptable if customers are clearly informed in advance that these will be calculated at checkout.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: "Nagging" designs that repeatedly prompt users to take the same action, and excessive consent interactions that create "click fatigue," leading to unintentional agreement.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Fake or manipulated reviews, endorsements, or star ratings.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Personalisation or targeting based on sensitive data, emotional profiling, or inferred vulnerabilities.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Please read the Appendix for detailed views and recommendations related to digital fairness.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: The views within this document are representative of Inter IKEA Group and Ingka Group. For more information, please contact:

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Gustav Wiigh Public Affairs Leader, Inter IKEA Group gustav.wiigh@inter.ikea.com

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: Giacomo Pizza EU Public Affairs Leader, Ingka Group giacomo.pizza@ingka.ikea.com

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: About IKEA IKEA offers well-designed, functional, and affordable, high-quality home furnishing, produced with care for people and the environment. There are several companies with different owners, working under the IKEA Brand, all sharing the same vision: to create a better everyday life for the many people. IKEA was founded in Sweden in 1943.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: About Inter IKEA Group Inter IKEA Group includes Inter IKEA Systems B.V., IKEA of Sweden AB, IKEA Supply AG, IKEA Industry AB, and related businesses. Inter IKEA Holding B.V. is the holding company for the Inter IKEA Group.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: About Ingka Group With IKEA retail operations on 31 markets, Ingka Group is the largest IKEA retailer and represents about 90% of IKEA retail sales. It is a strategic partner to develop and innovate the IKEA business and help define common IKEA strategies. Ingka Group owns and operates IKEA sales channels under franchise agreements with Inter IKEA Systems B.V. It has three business areas: IKEA Retail, Ingka Investments and Ingka Centres. Read more on www.ingka.com.

section_path: IKEA position on the Digital Fairness Act
Harmful digital practices to explicitly prohibit
paragraphs.text: About the IKEA franchise System The IKEA retail business is operated through a franchise system with franchisees that are authorised to market and sell the IKEA product range within specified geographical territories. Inter IKEA Systems B.V. is the owner of the IKEA Concept and worldwide IKEA franchisor, who also assigns different IKEA companies to develop the range, supply products and deliver communication solutions. Today, 12 different groups of companies own and operate IKEA sales channels under franchise agreements with Inter IKEA Systems B.V.

We design digital experiences with the same care as our physical ones. Through our global digital design system we ensure equal visibility and ease of choice in all user interfaces. We review our cookie banners and checkout flows to ensure customers can decline or adjust consent as easily as accepting it. For example, in several of our markets we have simplified the “reject all” options into a single click, avoiding the multi-step consent patterns that frustrate users. We have also identified improvement areas, such as ensuring that “bestseller” tags and visual colour cues (for example red price tags) cannot be misinterpreted as part of a discount if they are not.

We support clear, evidence-based definitions of dark patterns to distinguish manipulative designs from legitimate user experience elements. To ensure coherence across the EU legal landscape, these definitions should align with existing legislation (including the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, Digital Services Act, Digital Markets Act, AI Act, and GDPR) particularly regarding consent, profiling, and platform obligations. The Digital Fairness Act could build on or amend the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive to explicitly address dark patterns and unfair personalisation practices as misleading or aggressive commercial behaviour. This approach would strengthen enforcement while providing consistency for businesses and clarity for consumers.
Explicitly define and prohibit dark patterns in EU consumer law, especially around consent, subscription cancellation, and purchasing.
Require parity in consent design so that accept and reject options are equally visible and completed in one click.
Ensure design fairness obligations are integrated into compliance by design frameworks under existing law, and coherent with the UCPD, DSA, DMA, AI Act and GDPR.

We believe technology should serve people's needs, not their attention spans. We take great care to ensure our online shopping experience supports better choices free from manipulative features that push customers towards purchases they did not mean to make. Our websites and apps avoid infinite scrolling and autoplay, and video content plays only when the user chooses to engage. Internal design reviews regularly assess that interfaces do not over-stimulate or pressure users, particularly children. We are phasing out features such as frequent discount countdowns that can unintentionally mimic addictive mechanics.

Enforcement only works when the law is clearly defined. Specific rules on manipulative and attention-driven design, aligned with the Digital Services Act and Audiovisual Media Services Directive, would help authorities act faster and more consistently. Building on UNICEF's insights, we also believe the Digital Fairness Act should embed the impact on children's rights as a central consideration when defining and enforcing what constitutes addictive design. Additionally, industry guidance and voluntary design principles can usefully complement binding rules, helping designers interpret and apply fairness principles effectively.
Ban default use of addictive design features such as infinite scrolling, autoplay, and manipulative notifications.
Require clear opt-in controls for features affecting time, attention, or engagement.
Apply special protections to services likely accessed by children, including automatic limits or opt-outs.
Align obligations with the DSA and Audiovisual Media Services Directive to ensure consistency.
Embed accessibility standards so controls and warnings are usable by people with visual or cognitive disabilities.

We do not use or promote gambling-like mechanisms such as loot boxes or pay-to-progress features. The proliferation of these features in the wider digital ecosystem impacts consumer trust overall. Customers who face manipulative pricing or rewards elsewhere bring those expectations with them, even to fair platforms. The Digital Fairness Act should clarify that such practices fall under unfair commercial behaviour when aimed at consumers, in particular vulnerable users and children.
Ban the use of gambling-like features such as loot boxes for children and introduce spending-limit controls for all users.
Require full cost transparency and real-currency equivalents for any digital purchase.
Clarify platform accountability to ensure parental and consumer controls are active by default.
Coordinate enforcement across consumer, gaming, and media authorities to avoid fragmentation.

At IKEA, personalisation means shaping experiences around people's real needs, preferences, and contexts, not influencing them to act. We personalise only when it brings clear value and is relevant to both customers and our business. For Inter IKEA Group, the approach is guided by four principles: enhance emotional connections, be transparent, put the customer at the heart, and serve the many. We believe the Digital Fairness Act should support responsible personalisation, that empowers them with meaningful choice, protects their privacy in line with the GDPR, and ensures data-driven experiences remain fair, transparent, and consistent with the Consumer Rights Directive.

Our personalisation model tailors the way we communicate with customers, the content we share, and the timing of our messages to be more useful and relevant to people's everyday lives. In our Home Smart products, for example, data is pseudonymised and processed locally wherever possible, ensuring personalisation respects privacy and remains firmly under the user's control.

When identifying new personalisation opportunities, we evaluate them against the IKEA Retail Strategy to ensure they strengthen the IKEA Brand, reach more of the many, and deliver growth responsibly. Every scenario is assessed for strategic fit, potential impact, and feasibility, so that personalisation always serves our customers.
Encourage responsible personalisation practices that bring clear, measurable value to consumers.
Clarify in what situations personalised recommendations risk becoming manipulative, and which types of data may be appropriately used.
Ban personalisation or targeting of children and the use of sensitive or inferred vulnerability data.
Provide centrally and easily available, plain-language explanations of how personalisation works, which data is used, and how customers can control or turn it off at any time.
Align enforcement between the GDPR, DSA, AI Act, and Consumer Rights Directive to ensure coherence and avoid overlaps or enforcement gaps.

AI agents are emerging as part of the online shopping experience. As these tools become more autonomous and start interacting directly with users and digital interfaces, they bring new ethical challenges. Without clear guardrails, AI systems can blur responsibility for actions, consent, and data use, leading to decisions that consumers neither intended nor fully understand.

We see clear concerns with agentic AI features such as auto-add to cart or automated checkout. These can unintentionally lead to purchases people did not mean to make, particularly for customers with disabilities or limited digital literacy. They also increase operational and support costs for businesses when customers try to reverse unintended actions. We therefore recommend that the Digital Fairness Act establishes explicit safeguards to ensure AI agents act in ways that protect consumers' interests and keep them fully in control of their decisions.
Require explicit consent and informed confirmation before an AI system suggests or completes a purchase.
Ensure clear and simple undo options for all AI-driven actions affecting spending, subscriptions, or data use.
Define clear accountability and liability between developers, traders, and platforms when AI acts on behalf of a consumer.
Align the Digital Fairness Act with the AI Act, the Digital Marketing Act and Digital Services Act to ensure coherent rules.
Require that all AI-assisted interactions are designed to be understandable and accessible for everyone, including people with disabilities.

Influencer marketing plays a growing role in shaping how people discover products. When we work with influencers, partnerships are clearly disclosed and carefully reviewed to reflect our values. For example, IKEA Sweden consistently uses clear ad labels such as "#partnership" or "#ad", helping audiences distinguish paid collaborations from organic content. This distinction between commercial and non-commercial content is particularly important to protect children, as native and influencer content can obscure commercial intent.

Across Europe, disclosure standards remain inconsistent at the national level, and there is uncertainty around when influencers qualify as traders or how far brands' responsibility extends. This inconsistency creates challenges for global brands like ours, operating under one set of
UKEA

shared values across many different national rules. Compliance is made more complex for companies acting responsibly and allows non-compliant actors to gain an unfair advantage. The Digital Fairness Act has the potential to clarify the responsibilities between influencers, agencies, and brands, ensuring accountability matches who controls the content and benefits from it.
Define when influencers qualify as traders under EU law and harmonise simple, visible disclosure rules across platforms and Member States.
Clarify accountability between influencers, brands, and agencies so responsibility matches control over content.

Encourage certification and training programmes for influencers to improve understanding of disclosure rules, building on best-practice models such as the Dutch SRO/ SRC (Stichting Reclame Code) certification scheme.
Empower Advertising Standards Agencies to effectively govern platforms that enable influencer marketing.
Introduce proportionate safeguards for children by limiting influencer marketing for harmful or misleading products.

We do not use dynamic or personalised pricing, and the total price, including delivery and mandatory fees, is always visible before checkout. We explicitly avoid practices that change or increase prices during the purchasing process or add unexpected fees after the customer has initiated checkout. Such tactics can make the customers feel pressured or trapped and undermine trust in digital commerce.

At the same time, we recognise that some costs (such as delivery or installation) can only be accurately calculated once the customer's basket and location are known. In these cases, transparency means clearly informing customers early in the process that these costs will be determined at checkout and showing how they are calculated. We also ensure that product prices are never inflated or bundled with mandatory service costs unless this is clearly disclosed and separated. Customers should always understand whether a price includes additional services and have the option to choose or remove them where applicable.

We avoid countdowns, "starting-from" prices, and similar tactics that create artificial urgency or obscure total costs. Internal design reviews have shown even small visual choices, such as the colour or placement of "new lower price" labels, can unintentionally signal discounts. We make sure to refine our pricing presentation to ensure it always reflects real, recent changes and cannot be confused with promotions. Clear, harmonised EU rules are needed to protect consumers and ensure clear pricing becomes the market standard.

Ban price changes during the purchase process or the addition of unexpected fees once checkout has begun. The price shown at the start of purchase should remain valid until payment completion, except where certain variable costs (e.g., delivery or installation) are transparently calculated based on basket size or location.
Ban drip pricing and require all mandatory fees, including delivery or environmental costs, to be disclosed before purchase.
Prohibit inflating product prices with undisclosed service costs and require clear separation between product and service pricing to ensure customers understand what they are paying for.
Require that discount and reference prices reflect genuine, recent, and verifiable changes in price.
Prohibit misleading "starting-from" or countdown offers that create false urgency or scarcity.
Strengthen coordinated enforcement through the Consumer Protection Cooperation Network to ensure consistent protection across the EU.

Our ambition is to make every service and membership simple to understand, enter into, and end. We review how digital sign-ups and cancellations are carried out to remove unnecessary steps and unclear wording. For example, in several markets we have updated the IKEA Family subscription flow so that leaving the programme takes the same number of clicks as joining it. We are also simplifying checkout and service pages so that people always know what they agree to before confirming a purchase. And when things do go wrong, customers are always able to reach a real person for help.
Make cancellation as simple, visible, and quick as sign-up.
Ban automatic renewals and charges after trials without clear, informed consent.
Require advance reminders before subscriptions renew or convert to paid plans.
Ensure contract terms use clear, readable language that customers can easily understand.
Guarantee at least one direct way to reach human support for billing or contract issues.
End-of-document
